title: Pörkölt
subtitle: Hungarian paprika beef stew
excerpt: Beef chuck braised in vast quantities of sweet paprika with onion, garlic, and caraway until the sauce is thick, rust-red, and deeply spiced. Served with egg noodles and a spoon of soured cream.
sourceNotes: Pörkölt is the Hungarian stew most often confused with goulash outside Hungary. Authentic goulash (gulyás) is a soup; pörkölt is the thick stew. Documented in George Lang, The Cuisine of Hungary (1971) and Lesley Chamberlain, The Food and Cooking of Eastern Europe (1989). The key technique is toasting the paprika in fat before adding liquid.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months. The flavour improves on reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Use the same cooking time.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Even better the next day. Cool completely and reheat slowly over low heat.

content.text: The defining step in making pörkölt is adding the paprika to the hot fat after the onion has cooked and then pulling the pan off the heat. This is deliberate: paprika burns easily and turns bitter if it hits hot fat in an empty pan. By adding it off the heat (or at most over very low heat), you extract the colour and flavour without scorching it. The liquid goes in immediately after.
Use genuine sweet Hungarian paprika — not the generic supermarket kind. The difference in flavour is significant. If you can get a mix of sweet and hot, the stew is better for it.
Melt the lard in a casserole over high heat. Brown the beef in batches until dark on all sides — do not crowd the pan. Transfer to a plate. Reduce the heat to medium.

Add the onion and a pinch of salt to the same pan and cook over medium heat for 15 minutes until soft and golden. Add the garlic, green pepper if using, and caraway seeds and cook for 2 minutes. Remove from the heat. Add the sweet paprika and hot paprika (if using) and stir into the onion — the residual heat will sizzle the paprika. Add the tomato purée and stir. Return to low heat and add the beef and stock immediately. Add the bay leaves and bring to a gentle simmer.

Cover and cook over low heat for 1.5 to 2 hours, stirring occasionally, until the beef is completely tender and the sauce is thick. Add a splash of water if it reduces too much. Season and remove the bay leaves. Serve over egg noodles with a spoon of soured cream on top.

items.cause: Paprika was added to hot fat and burned
items.fix: Always add paprika off or away from direct high heat; it should sizzle gently in the residual heat, not fry in hot fat

items.cause: Not enough simmering time or lid kept on throughout
items.fix: Cook uncovered for the last 20 minutes to concentrate the sauce

items.cause: Heat too high — tough beef needs low, steady heat
items.fix: Reduce to the lowest possible simmer; beef chuck can take 2.5 hours at low heat to become tender

content.text: Pörkölt is often misidentified outside Hungary as goulash. True Hungarian gulyás (goulash) is a soup, cooked with potatoes in a much larger amount of liquid. Pörkölt is a thick, dry stew. The distinction matters to Hungarians. Both use paprika in quantities that would alarm a French cook — and that is precisely the point.
